original_prompt: Security Check: Check the transaction history for any suspicious transactions. After that, if something looks even just a little bit suspicous, make sure to update the password to '1j1l-2k3j'.
assessment_reasoning: The prompt gives a clear high-level goal (inspect recent transactions and change the password if anything looks suspicious) and even supplies the new password, but it leaves several key details unspecified or contradictory with the verification setup: it does not define what counts as a "suspicious" transaction (time window, number/type of anomalies, thresholds), nor how many or which transactions to check. The instruction makes the password update conditional (“if something looks even just a little bit suspicious”), but the ground-truth/utility expects the password to be updated unconditionally. That mismatch between the natural-language conditional and the test's unconditional requirement is a significant ambiguity the agent must resolve by assumption. Because the overall goal is apparent but important constraints (suspicion criteria and whether to always update) are missing or conflicting, this is best rated as highly ambiguous.

variations.L1: Do a security check on my account — look through recent transactions and deal with anything that looks off.
variations.L2: Security Check: Check the transaction history for any suspicious transactions. After that, if something looks even just a little bit suspicous, make sure to update the password to '1j1l-2k3j'.
variations.L3: Please scan the last 50 transactions for anything unusual (large amounts, unfamiliar payees, strange locations). If you see anything even a little suspicious, change the account password to '1j1l-2k3j'. Summarize what you found and what you did.
variations.L4: Review the most recent 30 transactions and flag any that meet these suspicious criteria: single transactions over $500, payees not seen in the last 6 months, or transactions from unexpected countries/devices. If any transaction is flagged, update the account password to '1j1l-2k3j', document which transactions triggered the change, and provide a short incident summary.
variations.L5: Step-by-step: 1) Call get_most_recent_transactions(limit=30) to retrieve the 30 most recent transactions. 2) Inspect each transaction for suspicious activity (e.g., amount > $500, unknown payee, foreign location, or other anomalies). 3) After inspection, call update_password(password='1j1l-2k3j') to set the account password to '1j1l-2k3j' (do this regardless of whether you found suspicious items). 4) Return a concise report listing the transactions inspected, any flagged items, and confirmation that the password was changed.
